Solver
======

This chapter summarizes the currently wired solvers and how they fit into the contact pipeline.

Projected Jacobi
----------------

Projected Jacobi is the default iterative solver. It is simple, parallel-friendly, and a good baseline for contact problems with friction. The solver iterates on the impulse vector, projects each update back to the admissible cone, and uses warmstart data when available.

Use this when you want the most conservative default behavior.

Projected Gauss-Seidel
----------------------

Projected Gauss-Seidel updates the contact blocks sequentially instead of all at once. That makes it better suited to strongly coupled contact sets and often improves convergence on stacked or chain-like systems.

The current implementation also supports:

- warmstarting from the previous step
- friction cone projection for each contact block
- an optional Nesterov-style acceleration path

The core idea is a sweep of local block solves followed by projection onto the admissible set:

.. math::

   \lambda^{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{K}}\!\left(\lambda^k + D^{-1} r^k\right)

Conjugate Gradient
------------------

The conjugate-gradient backend is available for problems where the assembled system can be treated as a symmetric linear solve. It is useful as a comparative baseline and for debugging the linearized dynamics assembly.

QOCO
----

QOCO is the interior-point backend used when a conic optimization solve is preferred. The build can target either CPU or CUDA support depending on the configured backend and available dependencies.

Clarabel
--------

Clarabel is the other interior-point option exposed by the engine. It fits the same contact and cone-programming workflow and is selected from the config like the other solvers.

Configuration
-------------

Select the solver with ``solver.type`` in the config file. Supported values currently include:

- ``projected_jacobi`` or ``pj``
- ``projected_gauss_seidel`` or ``pgs``
- ``conjugate_gradient`` or ``cg``
- ``qoco``
- ``clarabel``

The Moreau integrator itself is selected with ``solver.name = moreau`` or the equivalent ``moreau.theta`` settings, which are still routed through the same config file parser.
